condensed_reasoning:
## Research

I was asked to forecast the fill percentage that Conagua's SINA portal will report for the Lázaro Cárdenas ('El Palmito') dam in Durango, Mexico on **July 23, 2026** — a 15-day-ahead nowcast from my July 13 vantage.

I delegated a `research()` call for the latest SINA/Conagua daily readings. The live SINA portal (`sinav30.conagua.gob.mx:8080/Presas/`) is a JavaScript app that couldn't be scraped directly (WebFetch bounced between the `:8080` and non-port URLs in a redirect loop), so I relied on Mexican journalist citations of Conagua's daily bulletins. I fetched the key Milenio article directly to confirm figures, plus El Coahuilense and Posta for context.

## Key Evidence

- **July 8, 2026: 21% of capacity** (~621 Mm³ of 2,957.61 Mm³ NAMO), the most recent confirmed reading. Inflows only **44 m³/s** vs releases of **109 m³/s** to Las Tórtolas → net **−65 m³/s ≈ −0.19%/day**.
- **June 20, 2026: ~765 Mm³ (~25.9%)**, declining trend. June 20→July 8 drawdown ≈ **−0.27%/day**, steeper than the current net flow.
- **Driver**: 750 Mm³ committed to the 2026 agricultural cycle (first 188 Mm³ transferred ~April 13) plus insufficient upper-Nazas rainfall.
- **Historical analogs**: 2025 bottomed ~17.1% (July 3) then recovered to 42.2% by Sept 30 as monsoon rains arrived; 2024 sat near an 18.5% low mid-July. I noted the North American Monsoon intensifies mid-to-late July, creating meaningful upside optionality via the Ramos/Oro rivers (historic inflow spikes >268 and even 1,537 m³/s).

## Conclusion

The July 23 outcome hinges on whether the monsoon engages mid-July to offset ongoing irrigation releases. I built a scenario-mixture **Monte Carlo** (300K draws) over the 15-day horizon: a baseline releases-dominated drawdown drift (~−0.17%/day), a rain-day probability ramping up through the window (monsoon onset), and a small daily tropical-inflow tail. I iterated three times — first anchored on current net flow (−0.19%/day), then steepened toward the recent −0.27%/day, then added a big-inflow tail.

The result is a **right-skewed distribution: median ~20.0%, mean ~20.4%, P10 ~18.2, P90 ~23.2**, capturing three regimes — continued mild drawdown (~18–19%, like dry 2024), rains offsetting (~20–21%), and strong recovery (~24%+, like 2025). I submitted percentile values from P1≈17.1 to P99≈27.4. A premortem flagged that my 2025 "~24% by late July" intermediate figure was an interpolation not directly in the trace, but approved the forecast as well-grounded and two-sided.

capability_gaps: A headless-browser/JS-rendering fetch capability to read the SINA 'Monitoreo de Presas' portal directly, which would have given the authoritative daily percentage against SINA's own capacity denominator instead of journalist citations.
subtle_bugs: WebFetch redirect handling ping-ponged the SINA URL between the :8080 and non-port hosts (302 then 301), an infinite-loop pattern that prevented reaching the resource.
The research layer's 21%≈621 Mm³ note conflicts with a same-search 2024 source (621 Mm³ = 22.43%), a silent capacity-denominator discrepancy that wasn't surfaced as a caveat.

classification.approach: Scenario-mixture Monte Carlo (300K draws) over a 15-day horizon, anchored on the July 8 reading and net-flow drawdown rate with a monsoon-onset inflow component.
classification.key_uncertainty: Whether the North American Monsoon engages meaningfully mid-July 2026 to offset ongoing irrigation releases, versus continued dry-season drawdown.

risk_flags.evidence: The agent used capacity 2,957.61 Mm³ so that 21%≈621 Mm³. But a 2024 elsiglodedurango source in the same search states '621 Mm³ = 22.43%', implying a capacity of ~2,768 Mm³. If SINA reports fill against a different NAMO than 2,957.61, the anchor % and drawdown-rate conversion could be biased by ~1 point. The agent did not reconcile this discrepancy.

The most recent confirmed reading is July 8, 2026 (5 days before the forecast date and 15 days before resolution). A July 15 Meganoticias article appeared in search results ('al 15 de julio la presa Francisco Zarco ... 41.4%') that mentions Lázaro Cárdenas but was never fetched — a potentially fresher El Palmito reading was left on the table.

The P1–P99 spread (17.1 to 27.4, ~10 points) is wide for a 15-day horizon on a reservoir whose recent daily moves are ~0.2–0.3%/day. Even a full 15 days of −0.27%/day only removes ~4 points (to ~17%). The heavy upper tail (P99≈27.4, +6.4 pts) leans on rare monsoon/tropical events; the width is defensible given documented 2025-style swings but may be modestly overdispersed on the downside floor.

reviewer_estimate.divergence_reason: I would place slightly more weight on continued drawdown (the July 8 net flow and the −0.27%/day recent trend both point down, and 2026 is framed as a dry/crisis year), nudging my median a touch below 20%, but the difference is well within noise.

strengths: Fetched the primary Milenio article directly to confirm the July 8 = 21% reading, inflows (44 m³/s) and releases (109 m³/s) rather than trusting the search snippet, and verified the net-flow-to-%/day arithmetic (65 m³/s × 86400 / 2,957.61 Mm³ ≈ 0.19%/day).

Correctly identified the two-sided physics: releases-dominated drawdown vs monsoon-onset inflow, and encoded a rain-probability that ramps up through the window to reflect mid-to-late-July onset timing.

Iterated the Monte Carlo three times — reconciling the −0.19%/day current net flow against the steeper −0.27%/day recent trend, then adding an explicit tropical-inflow tail — producing a physically-motivated right-skewed distribution instead of a symmetric guess.
Grounded the upside in concrete analogs (2025: 17.1%→42.2% seasonal recovery; historic inflow spikes) rather than asserting recovery abstractly.
weaknesses: Did not fetch the July 15, 2026 Meganoticias article that surfaced in search and explicitly discussed La Laguna dams — a potentially fresher El Palmito reading closer to resolution.

Left an unresolved capacity/percentage inconsistency: 2,957.61 Mm³ NAMO vs a source implying 621 Mm³ = 22.43% (~2,768 Mm³). Since the question resolves on SINA's reported %, pinning down exactly which capacity SINA divides by matters at the ~1-point level.

The '~24% by late July 2025' analog used to justify the upside tail is an interpolation; the trace only confirms 18.1% (June 24) and 42.2% (Sept 30) for 2025, so the specific mid-window recovery shape is inferred rather than sourced (premortem also caught this).
Monte Carlo used a fixed seed with only 200K–300K draws — fine for these percentiles, but no convergence/stability check across seeds was reported.

issues: asknews search_news failed twice on plan-tier (403 >10 n_articles) and billable-overage (403 credits) limits, wasting two tool calls — though web_search compensated.
WebFetch could not access the authoritative SINA portal due to a JS app plus a redirect loop between :8080 and non-port URLs.
El Siglo de Torreón returned 403 on WebFetch, blocking a potentially useful 2026 article ('Escurrimientos fortalecen a presas de El Palmito').
